preamble: The shift from the voiced bilabial stop [b] to the voiced alveolar stop [d] is known as coronalization — the replacement of a labial articulation with a coronal (tongue-front) articulation. This direction of change is typologically marked: most bilabial-coronal shifts go in the reverse direction (coronalization is rarer than labialization). It occurs most consistently in two contexts: (1) before front vowels, where the tongue body moves forward and can trigger a coronal onset; and (2) in child phonology, where alveolar [d] is an early-acquired stop that sometimes substitutes for the less-visible bilabial [b]. In Southern Bantu languages, alveolar alternants of labial consonants appear in certain morphological environments (e.g., passive constructions with a -w- suffix), yielding [dʒ] or [d] outputs from original [b]. Historically documented cases of b > d as a regular sound law are rare; the change most often appears as a sporadic or morphologically conditioned alternation.

examples.note: Labial consonants in some Southern Bantu languages shift toward coronal or palatal outputs in morphological contexts such as passive and applicative constructions, a process sometimes called alveolarization in Bantu phonology.
Coronal substitution is documented in developmental phonology: alveolar [d] is acquired before bilabial [b] in some children, leading to temporary b → d substitution as a phonological process.

sources: Maddieson, I. (2019). The sounds of the Bantu languages. In M. Van de Velde et al. (eds.), The Bantu Languages, 2nd ed. Routledge.
